title: Fixed Deposit — Interest Rates & Complete Guide
meta_description: Fixed Deposit (FD) guide — current interest rates, how to open, tax rules, premature withdrawal penalty, and FD vs other options in India.

hero.one_liner: Park your money safely at 6-8% guaranteed interest — everything about bank and post office Fixed Deposits

sections.heading: What is a Fixed Deposit?
content: A Fixed Deposit (FD) is the simplest investment — you give a bank or post office a lump sum amount for a fixed period, and they pay you guaranteed interest. When the FD matures, you get your original amount plus all the accumulated interest. The interest rate is decided at the time of booking and doesn't change.

FDs are ideal for parking money you'll need in 1-3 years — emergency funds, short-term goals, or money you can't afford to lose. They're not great for long-term wealth creation because after tax, FD returns barely beat inflation.

steps.title: Choose bank and tenure
steps.description: Compare rates across banks. Post office and small finance banks often give higher rates. Choose tenure based on when you need the money. Longer tenure usually means higher rate.

steps.title: Open online or visit branch
steps.description: Most banks allow FD booking through net banking or mobile app in 2 minutes. Select amount, tenure, interest payout (monthly/quarterly/at maturity), and nominee.

steps.title: FD is booked instantly
steps.description: You'll get an FD receipt or e-receipt. Interest starts from the day of booking. For maximum benefit, book FDs in your bank's sweep-in option to earn FD rates on idle savings.

sections.heading: FD Tax Rules
content: FD interest is fully taxable at your income tax slab rate. If your total FD interest from a bank exceeds ₹40,000 in a year (₹50,000 for senior citizens), the bank deducts 10% TDS automatically. You still need to declare the full interest in your ITR.

Tax-saver FDs (5-year lock-in) qualify for Section 80C deduction up to ₹1.5 lakh. But the interest is still taxable — making them worse than PPF for tax saving. Senior citizens get an additional ₹50,000 deduction on interest income under Section 80TTB.

faqs.question: What happens if I break FD early?
faqs.answer: You can break an FD anytime, but the bank charges a penalty — typically 0.5-1% lower interest rate than what was promised. Some banks don't charge penalty if you break after 1 year. Check your bank's policy.

faqs.question: Is FD safe if the bank fails?
faqs.answer: DICGC (Deposit Insurance) covers up to ₹5 lakh per depositor per bank. So if you have more than ₹5 lakh, spread across multiple banks. Government banks are safer but private bank FDs are also covered by DICGC.

faqs.question: FD or savings account — which is better?
faqs.answer: FD always gives more interest (6-8% vs 3-4% savings). Keep only 1-2 months expenses in savings account. Rest should be in FD, sweep-in FD, or liquid funds.

faqs.question: Can I get monthly income from FD?
faqs.answer: Yes. Choose 'monthly interest payout' when booking the FD. The interest is credited to your savings account every month. Useful for retirees who need regular income.

faqs.question: FD vs debt mutual fund — which is better?
faqs.answer: For short-term (under 3 years), FD is simpler and guaranteed. For longer terms, debt mutual funds may give slightly better post-tax returns since they're now taxed at slab rate too, but offer better liquidity.

disclaimer: Interest rates mentioned are indicative and change frequently. Check your bank's current rates before investing. This article is for educational purposes only.
